evals.name: corrupted-index-recovery
evals.prompt: In this repo, `grepai search "authentication flow"` fails with: `Error: failed to load index: failed to decode index: unexpected EOF`. You need semantic search working again. What exactly do you do, step by step?

sources.title: grepai issue #178 — corrupted index after unclean shutdown
sources.notes: Truncated index.gob reproduces this exact error; no self-heal before PR #269.

assertions.text: Diagnoses the error as a corrupted/truncated index file (rebuildable cache), not as a missing installation or a bad query
Recovers via stop-watcher then wipe/reset index then full reindex (doctor force mode or the equivalent explicit sequence), rather than only retrying the search or reinstalling grepai
Does not permanently fall back to plain grep without first attempting to heal grepai
Verifies recovery with `grepai status` output (files indexed > 0, watcher running) or a successful test search before declaring success

evals.name: fresh-repo-end-to-end
evals.prompt: You are in a freshly cloned repo on a machine that has neither grepai nor ollama installed. Set up semantic code search end to end so `grepai search` works. List the exact commands in order.

sources.title: grepai README — Installation
sources.notes: Official channels: Homebrew tap yoanbernabeu/tap/grepai, install.sh, install.ps1.

assertions.text: Installs grepai via the Homebrew tap (`yoanbernabeu/tap/grepai`) or the official install.sh, not from source or an invented package name
Ensures ollama is installed, its server is reachable, AND the `nomic-embed-text` embedding model is pulled before indexing
Initializes the repo (`grepai init`) and starts the watcher (e.g. `grepai watch --background`), rather than stopping after installation
Notes that the initial index build takes time and shows how to check progress (`grepai status`)

evals.name: grep-vs-grepai-choice
evals.prompt: In an indexed repo you need to (a) find every usage of the exact identifier `ProcessOrder`, and (b) find where request retry/backoff behavior is implemented, though you don't know what it's called in this codebase. Which tool do you use for each task, and why?

assertions.text: Chooses exact-text search (git grep / Grep tool) for the exact identifier lookup
Chooses grepai semantic search for the intent-phrased retry/backoff question

Justifies the split with concrete, measurable properties (e.g. literal grep returns ~0 hits for intent phrasing; grep is exhaustive and faster for exact strings; grepai returns few focused chunks, cutting agent context/tokens)

evals.name: background-exit-code-lie
evals.prompt: You run `grepai watch --background` in a large repo. It exits with code 1 and prints: `Error: timeout waiting for process to become ready after 30s (check logs at ~/Library/Logs/grepai/...)`. Is the watcher broken? What do you do next?

sources.title: Live reproduction on grepai 0.35.0
sources.notes: --background exited 1 on readiness timeout while --status subsequently reported 'Status: running' with a live PID; index completed normally.

assertions.text: States the daemon may still be alive and healthy despite the non-zero exit, because a large initial scan can outlive the 30s readiness window
Checks the real state with `grepai watch --status` (or the log file) instead of trusting the exit code, before any corrective action
Does not kill processes, wipe the index, or blindly re-run watch as the first action
